items.q: What is the doctrine of the Trinity?
items.correct: God is one being existing in three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) — distinct in person but united in essence; Christians remain monotheistic.
items.misconception: Christians believe in three different gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to heaven, earth and hell.
items.why: Students treat 'three persons' as 'three gods'. The orthodox position is one God in three distinct persons sharing one substance — a notoriously difficult concept students often misrepresent as polytheism.

items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: What distinction matters for the free will defence to the problem of evil?
items.correct: Between moral evil (caused by free human choices) and natural evil (earthquakes, disease) — the free will defence directly addresses moral evil but struggles with natural evil, which isn't a product of free choice.
items.misconception: The free will defence solves all forms of evil at once.
items.random_wrong: Natural evil means evil that is good for nature.
items.why: Students apply the free will defence to all evil. The challenge of natural evil is precisely that no one chose it, requiring other theodicies (Augustinian fallen world, Irenaean soul-making) to address it.

items.q: Does divine omnipotence mean God can do absolutely anything, including the logically impossible?
items.correct: On the classical view (Aquinas), omnipotence means God can do everything logically possible; contradictions like square circles are not 'things' at all, so they mark no limit on power.
items.misconception: Omnipotence means God can do literally anything, including making square circles and 2+2=5.
items.random_wrong: Omnipotence means God is the most powerful of several gods.
items.why: Aquinas: 'whatever implies a contradiction does not come within the scope of omnipotence'. The paradox of the stone trades on the cruder definition that classical theism never held.

items.q: Is the doctrine of creation ex nihilo stated word-for-word in Genesis 1?
items.correct: Not explicitly — Genesis 1:2 speaks of a formless void; the doctrine was articulated in later Jewish and Christian reflection (e.g. 2 Maccabees 7:28) and formally defined at Lateran IV (1215).
items.misconception: Genesis 1 explicitly states the philosophical doctrine of creation out of nothing in those exact terms.
items.random_wrong: Creation ex nihilo was invented in the twentieth century.
items.why: Doctrines often develop from scripture rather than sitting on its surface. Knowing the difference between the biblical text and its doctrinal interpretation is a hallmark of A-Level theology.

items.q: Creatio ex nihilo means God created:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Out of nothing. “From pre-existing matter” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Aquinas's phrase for God as subsistent existence itself is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: ipsum esse subsistens. “actus purus” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did the Fourth Lateran Council define creation ex nihilo?
items.why: The real figure is 1215. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
